headline: Malaysia's flood bill fell 32% in 2025. The bill was 10 times larger four years ago.
context: On 15 April 2026, DOSM reported total flood damage of RM636.9 million for 2025, down from RM933.4 million in 2024. The same DOSM series put 2021 at RM6.1 billion. Public infrastructure now carries 60% of the smaller 2025 bill.

cards.big: Malaysia's flood damage bill fell 32% in 2025 to RM636.9 million. The same DOSM series put 2021 at RM6.1 billion, ten times the 2025 number.
cards.sub: Inside the smaller total, public-asset damage rose 25% to RM380.2 million. Government audits show why infrastructure now carries the rising share.

cards.big: DOSM put total flood losses at RM636.9M for 2025, down 31.8% from RM933.4M in 2024 and equal to 0.03% of nominal GDP.
cards.sub: The figure counts direct damage to property and infrastructure for calendar 2025. The same DOSM series put 2021 at RM6.1 billion, ten times the 2025 number.

cards.big: Public-asset damage rose 25.3% to RM380.2M in 2025 while household losses fell 50.6% to RM183.8M. Public infrastructure now carries about 60% of the bill.
cards.sub: In 2024 it carried 33%. The State's share grew by RM77 million while household, farm and business damage all fell sharply.

cards.big: Government audits flag failures in the very projects meant to prevent the public-asset damage.
cards.sub: PAC found the national flood warning programme cost RM129 million and works at 5.6% accuracy. The Auditor-General logged 145 of 712 water projects delayed at RM6.15 billion.

cards.big: Same states top the damage table every year. Same projects miss every deadline. A number narrower than the cost.
Think of a household whose repair bill drops because the dam meant to protect it now leaks straight into the public works budget.
cards.sub: Less household pain. A larger public claim. A dam nobody finished fixing.

cards.big: The bill fell. The bill was ten times larger four years ago. The infrastructure meant to prevent the next one cost RM129 million and works 5.6% of the time.
